summary: Joined Charter Communications in March 2004 after a job fair at EarthLink — I'd hoped to become a field installer, but my customer service and technical support background at EarthLink landed me in the call center instead. Over the next two years I grew from customer service representative into technical support and eventually Retention Specialist, combining Internet/cable troubleshooting with the business side of keeping customers on service. Being fluently bilingual let me support Spanish-speaking customers who often struggled to find technical help in their own language. The retention role taught me that solving the technical problem wasn't always enough — I had to understand why a customer was frustrated and balance troubleshooting with business objectives like retention and upgrades. The role ended in March 2006 when Charter offered severance packages to cut costs amid merger talks and a weakening economy. This was the first job where I realized technical knowledge alone wasn't enough — communicating clearly and earning someone's trust mattered just as much as fixing the problem.
highlights: Grew from customer service representative into technical support and eventually Retention Specialist over two years, combining troubleshooting, sales, and customer communication in a single role.
Supported Spanish-speaking customers as a bilingual representative, resolving Internet and cable issues and providing service in their own language when few others could.
Balanced technical problem-solving with business retention goals, learning to listen for the real source of a customer's frustration before proposing a solution.
Navigated Charter's industry consolidation and a 2006 cost-cutting workforce reduction, leaving with a severance package — an early lesson in staying adaptable through organizational change.

Realized here, for the first time, that technical knowledge alone wasn't enough — clear communication and earned trust mattered just as much as fixing the problem, a principle that still shapes how I support users, executives, and technical teams today.
